title: Degrees of Comparison
definition: Adjectives and adverbs have three degrees of comparison: Positive (basic quality), Comparative (comparing two things), and Superlative (comparing three or more things).
formula: Positive: tall | Comparative: taller than | Superlative: the tallest
rules: One-syllable words: add '-er'/'-est' — tall, taller, tallest.
Two-syllable words ending in 'y': change y to i and add '-er'/'-est' — happy, happier, happiest.
Longer words (2+ syllables): use 'more'/'most' — beautiful, more beautiful, most beautiful.
Irregular forms: good → better → best; bad → worse → worst; far → farther/further → farthest/furthest.
Use 'than' with comparatives: She is taller than me.
Use 'the' with superlatives: She is the tallest in class.
examples: This bag is heavier than that one.
She is the smartest student in class.
This movie is more interesting than the last one.
He is the best player on the team.
Delhi is colder than Chennai in winter.
This is the most beautiful place I have ever seen.

commonMistakes.wrong: She is more taller than him.
commonMistakes.why: Don't use 'more' with an '-er' comparative form together.

commonMistakes.wrong: This is the most tallest building.
commonMistakes.why: Don't combine 'most' with an '-est' superlative form.
'Good' is irregular: good → better → best, not 'goodest'.

quiz.question: Choose the correct comparative of 'happy':
quiz.explanation: Change 'y' to 'i' and add '-er' for two-syllable words ending in y.

quiz.question: This road is ___ (wide) than that one.

quiz.sentence: She is the most smartest girl.
quiz.explanation: Don't combine 'most' with an '-est' superlative form.
